title: Signal Flag Drill
desc: The exercise line is practicing silent command work, which means the flags have to be faster than the voices.

steps.text:
Mipedian drills on Sha-Kree Flats are not loud unless something has already gone wrong. This morning's exercise is a signal run: scattered teams moving on silent flag commands across open sand, learning how to read distance, angle, and urgency without relying on a shouted order. From far off it looks almost ceremonial, narrow bursts of color rising and falling in a place where the horizon is usually the only line worth noticing.

The drill caller hands you a code slate and points between the upper signal frame and the ground box of folded pennants. "Take the high stand and repeat my calls if you trust your timing," she says. "Or work the color box and keep the sequence clean if you prefer to prevent mistakes before someone blames the wind."

options.label: Take the upper frame and relay the flag calls
Work the ground station and manage the pennant sequence

steps.text:
From the upper frame the whole drill field lies open beneath you, each squad a small moving problem waiting for color to tell it what to become next. The caller raises one pennant, then two, then a fast crossing sequence that means split, turn, and regroup on the lee side of marker three. Your job is to repeat each pattern without lag and without embellishment, because on Sha-Kree Flats even half a heartbeat of uncertainty can turn a clean drill into a lesson in dust.

By the third sequence your arms have settled into the rhythm and the squads below are moving like something jointed rather than separate. One team misses a turn, corrects immediately, and earns a glare from the instructor strong enough to cross a mile of sand. The caller lowers the final set and studies the field through narrowed eyes. "Good," she says. "Fast enough that they hated it, clear enough that they obeyed it." In drill language, that is nearly praise.

options.label: Lower the pennants and let the field reset

steps.text:
The ground station is less glamorous and more dangerous in its own way, because the whole drill depends on the right flag being in the right hand at the right second. The pennants are rolled by color and edge-cut so they can be recognized by touch: long black stripe for hold, forked red hem for split, weighted blue tassel for wheel left, plain yellow square for advance. The caller speaks only in clipped sequence numbers while you place each pennant where it must be before her hand reaches for it.

Most errors here do not look like errors at first. A cloth folded one turn too tight, a tassel buried under the wrong color, a code slate shifted by an inch. You prevent all of them and the drill flows because of it, which means no one notices until the caller reaches the end of the exercise without once swearing at the box. She looks down at the sorted cloths and says, "Excellent. The wind tried to confuse us and failed." That, from a Mipedian drill caller, is a small triumph.

options.label: Roll the pennants clean and close the signal box
